channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Challenge in the U.S.-Iran Talks: Both Sides Demand Victory
item.description: Washington and Tehran would need to defend any potential deal as a win for their side. And each has a leader whose approach to talks is vexing mediators.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon, Farnaz Fassihi and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A rally in Tehran on Monday after Iran and Israel exchanged fire.

item.title: Trump Wants to Call the Shots. But in Iran, He Keeps Hitting His Limits.
item.description: President Trump is grappling with his own version of the sort of Middle East crisis that beset his predecessors, and that he promised to avoid.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump insists that he has not violated his campaign pledge to avoid “endless wars,” even though a conflict he called “a little excursion” in March has entered its fourth month.

item.title: Crew Is Rescued After U.S. Helicopter Goes Down Near Strait of Hormuz
item.description: It was not immediately clear what led to the incident, which came at a time when the cease-fire in the war has grown tenuous.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A U.S. Army Apache helicopter went down near the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: The Holdup at the Center of the Iran Talks, and Trump’s Baseless New Claims of Voter Fraud
item.description: Plus, “I’m busy, but you can talk to my A.I. twin.”
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Erika Solomon, Devlin Barrett, Casey Newton and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has repeatedly said that the United States and Iran were coming close to a deal to end the war. It is not clear that they are actually any closer at the moment.

item.title: Trump Previews Fall Strategy With Baseless Claims of California Vote Fraud
item.description: The president is using the slow count of mail ballots in California to renew his effort to cast doubt on election outcomes he doesn’t like, despite a lack of evidence of any widespread fraud.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman
item.media:description: By baselessly framing the rise of a candidate for Los Angeles mayor as a Democratic scam, President Trump has extended his long-running project to erode public faith in elections.

item.title: Nithya Raman Secures Second LA Mayor Spot, Ending Spencer Pratt’s Run
item.description: Nithya Raman, a progressive Democrat who entered the mayoral race at the last minute, secured the second spot to face Mayor Karen Bass in November. Her comeback sets up an intraparty battle.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler

item.title: Democratic Group Kicks Off $50 Million Midterm Blitz in Conservative Strongholds
item.description: American Bridge is hoping that Republicans are vulnerable in parts of the country that had been exceedingly tough terrain for Democrats in recent elections.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: American Bridge is kicking off a roughly $50 million advertising campaign targeting Republicans in more than a dozen House districts and four Senate races.

item.title: Will Platner Scandals Dampen Democratic Vote? What to Watch in Tuesday’s Primaries.
item.description: Any indication of tepid support for Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, will be seen as a warning sign about his chances against Senator Susan Collins, the Republican incumbent.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine who is seeking to challenge Senator Susan Collins in the fall, has been mired in scandals in recent weeks.

item.title: Night With the Knicks: Rancor, Jubilation and an Unusual Loss
item.description: The night began with frustration and boos, and not because of the Knicks. But by the end of Game 3 of the N.B.A. finals, the team had slightly deflated some spirits, too.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar
item.media:description: President Trump’s presence at Game 3 of the N.B.A. finals at Madison Square Garden shifted the vibe both inside and outside the arena.

item.title: He Gave It All to Help the Knicks Win in 1973. He’s Still in Pain Today.
item.description: Earl “the Pearl” Monroe was part of the last championship team. Now 81, he has thoughts on what really matters.
item.dc:creator: David Waldstein
item.category: Earl Monroe New Renaissance Basketball School
item.media:description: In the deciding game of the 1973 N.B.A. championship, Earl Monroe drives past a Laker. The Knicks won their second title that night, May 10, 1973.

item.title: The Ex-Mayor of Arcadia, Her Boyfriend and the Chinese Government
item.description: Eileen Wang pleaded guilty to acting as a foreign agent. But what could Beijing want from the mayor of a small California city known as the “Chinese Beverly Hills”?
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:description: Eileen Wang, the former mayor of Arcadia, Calif., exiting federal court after pleading guilty last month.

item.title: In the Hybrid A.I.-Human Work Force, Who Will Actually Thrive?
item.description: A panel of experts explains how job seekers should prepare for the future of work.

item.title: Trump Nominates Blanche for Attorney General, Setting Up Confirmation Fight
item.description: As acting attorney general, Todd Blanche has shown a willingness to execute the president’s maximalist demands. Whether the Senate will confirm him remains unclear.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche’s role in the attempted creation of President Trump’s $1.8 billion payout fund gave rise to tensions with Senate Republicans, who will now have to decide whether to confirm him.

item.title: Pentagon Again Revises Religious Categories for Troops
item.description: The Defense Department made the change after lawmakers objected to its original list, which did not include the Latter-day Saints among traditions labeled Christian.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
item.media:description: Senator Mike Lee of Utah objected to a Pentagon policy memo excluding Mormons from being categorized as Christian.

item.title: The Idea of Womanhood Drawing Big Crowds to a Small Town
item.description: Tourists are flocking to Midway, Utah. The Ballerina Farm Store, from the influencer whose brand revolves around motherhood and farm life, is a reason.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Yee
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of friends filmed themselves before downing turmeric ginger shots outside the Ballerina Farm Store in Midway, Utah.

item.title: Infantino’s Yearslong Effort to Woo Trump for the World Cup
item.description: Gianni Infantino, the FIFA president, has unabashedly courted the president’s favor. Soccer officials privately ask, who really benefits?
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Rebecca R. Ruiz
item.media:description: President Trump with Gianni Infantino, FIFA’s president, at the White House in 2019.

item.title: Somali Referee Says His World Cup Dream Is Dashed After U.S. Denies Entry
item.description: “I had the right papers and everything,” Omar Abdulkadir Artan said in his first interview since he was turned back. He would have been the first Somali to referee a game in the tournament.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:description: Omar Abdulkadir Artan, right, during an Africa Cup of Nations match in Morocco last year.

item.title: Ukraine’s Neighbors Seek Shelter as More and More Drones Fly Overhead
item.description: Drones launched by both Russia and Ukraine are veering off course, menacing countries that are not at war.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins
item.media:credit: Andrej Vasilenko for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ligita Visockiene, the principal of a primary school in Vilnius, Lithuania, in a room the school uses as a shelter. The school has detailed plans for what to do in the event of attack but, she added, “We never thought we would have to actually use the plan.”

item.title: NASA Will Announce New Artemis III Astronauts and an Update on Its Moon Program
item.description: The agency will announce the crew of Artemis III on Tuesday. But will the mission be ready to fly in 2027?
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: Why a U.K. By-Election in Makerfield Could Topple Prime Minister Keir Starmer
item.description: Andy Burnham, the mayor of Greater Manchester, is running for a seat in Parliament. It’s a vital step in a campaign to oust his party colleague Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Andy Burnham launching his special election campaign last month. He was a member of Parliament for more than a decade before becoming mayor of Greater Manchester.

item.title: Vance Blames Migrant ‘Invasion’ for UK Stabbing
item.description: British officials accused Vice President JD Vance of trying to “stir up division” in his comments on the murder of Henry Nowak, whose killer was sentenced to life in prison last week.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Tributes for Henry Nowak outside the Portswood Police Station in Southampton, southern England, last week.

item.title: One of Pope Leo’s Best Friends Works in Spain. They Talk About Cars.
item.description: Pope Leo and Armando Jesús Lovera have known each other for decades. They have watched World Cup games together, gone on road trips and once searched for a teddy bear for Mr. Lovera’s future wife.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz
item.media:credit: Gianfranco Tripodo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Armando Jesús Lovera in Valladolid, Spain, at the local headquarters of the Augustinian order.

item.title: What Happens When ‘Star Wars’ Replaces Mozart?
item.description: Across the United States, orchestras are programming more live performances of movie soundtracks in a bid for box office revenue.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.category: Wolf Trap National Park for the Performing Arts (Fairfax County, Va)
item.media:credit: Nate Smallwood for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Star Wars” fans at Heinz Hall in Pittsburgh, where the Pittsburgh Symphony Orchestra recently performed a live soundtrack for “The Empire Strikes Back.”

item.title: The Gustavo Dudamel Era in Los Angeles Approaches Its End
item.description: Gustavo Dudamel’s last concerts at Walt Disney Concert Hall as the Los Angeles Philharmonic’s maestro were a symbol of his impact after 17 years.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Jake Michaels for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel, whose tenure as the music and artistic director of the Los Angeles Philharmonic concludes this summer.

item.title: If Only There Was a World Cup for Complaining About the World Cup
item.description: There is a lot to gripe about. There’s also a lot to like.
item.dc:creator: Bill Saporito
item.media:credit: Illustration by Sam Whitney/The New York Times

item.title: Word Choices
item.description: We explore six sentences that have shaped the American story.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Late Night Roasts Trump for Walking Out on ‘Meet the Press’
item.description: Jon Stewart called the president’s interview his “worst nightmare: a woman who won’t stop asking pertinent questions.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
